workflow.workflow_name: RIA code-of-ethics personal trading exception review
workflow.persona: Internal product strategist mapping the code-of-ethics exception workflow used by a compliance team reviewing employee personal trading and reporting issues
workflow.entry_condition: The adviser is running a periodic code-of-ethics review cycle or has received new employee trading, holdings, acknowledgment, or preclearance data that must be checked for exceptions.
workflow.completion_condition: Exceptions are triaged, any violations or waivers are adjudicated by the CCO, and the resulting records and follow-up actions are archived.
workflow_notes: This repo still treats each workflow slice as a locked artifact, not a multi-workflow product surface.
This third slice stays inside RIA compliance but changes the work shape again from filing and marketing review into recurring exception monitoring and supervisory adjudication.
Representative internal artifacts are included as workflow inputs, not as fabricated evidence.
Source evidence is limited to SEC rulemaking, risk alerts, form instructions, and examination-priority materials.
Firm-specific sanction matrices, restricted-list thresholds, and escalation routing are treated as local configuration, not unresolved scoring uncertainty.

sources.title: Final Rule: Investment Adviser Codes of Ethics
sources.summary: Adopted rule 204A-1 requiring adviser codes of ethics, periodic access-person holdings and transaction reports, pre-approval for IPOs and limited offerings, written acknowledgments, and related recordkeeping.

sources.title: Compliance Alert (July 2008)
sources.summary: Highlights common personal-trading deficiencies and effective practices such as duplicate confirms, current restricted lists, documented preclearance, centralized monitoring, annual certifications, and documented exceptions.

sources.title: The Five Most Frequent Compliance Topics Identified in OCIE Examinations of Investment Advisers
sources.summary: Identifies the code-of-ethics rule as a frequent examination topic, including failures to identify all access persons and failures to collect or review required holdings and transaction reports on time.

sources.title: Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues
sources.summary: Describes common code-of-ethics deficiencies, including incomplete access-person identification, missing pre-approval controls, weak review evidence, missing acknowledgments, and employee trading in restricted securities.

sources.title: Appendix C Part 2 of Form ADV
sources.summary: Requires advisers to describe their code of ethics, offer a copy to clients on request, and explain conflicts created by adviser or related-person personal trading in the same securities recommended to clients.

sources.title: SEC Division of Examinations Announces 2026 Priorities
sources.summary: Says examinations will continue to assess the effectiveness of adviser compliance programs, whether policies are implemented and enforced, and whether annual compliance reviews address core conflict and trading risks.

artifacts.name: Access-person roster and role matrix
artifacts.description: Roster of supervised persons, role attributes, related entities, and current access-person designation logic.

artifacts.name: Brokerage feed and duplicate-confirm map
artifacts.description: Mapping of employee brokerage accounts, duplicate-confirm delivery, feed coverage, and known manual-reporting gaps.

artifacts.name: Quarterly transaction and annual holdings package
artifacts.description: Combined set of quarterly transaction reports, annual holdings reports, initial holdings reports, and supporting account statements.

artifacts.name: Code delivery and acknowledgment log
artifacts.description: Log of code distributions, amendment notices, written acknowledgments, and annual certification status.

artifacts.name: Restricted-list and watchlist register
artifacts.description: Current do-not-trade, watchlist, and blackout-period controls used to screen employee trading activity.

artifacts.name: IPO, limited-offering, and preclearance request log
artifacts.description: Requests, approvals, denials, expiration dates, and supporting notes for personal-trading preclearance decisions.

artifacts.name: Code-of-ethics exception triage queue
artifacts.description: Structured list of missing reports, late acknowledgments, restricted-list hits, unmatched trades, and other flagged exceptions.

artifacts.name: Client trade blotter comparison snapshot
artifacts.description: Selected client-order and allocation data used to compare flagged employee trades against client trading activity and blackout rules.

artifacts.name: Exception review memo and remediation draft
artifacts.description: Reviewer draft summarizing the facts, likely policy issue, remediation options, training needs, and any disclosure follow-up.

artifacts.name: Violation, sanction, and retention log
artifacts.description: Final record of violations, waivers, sanctions, remediation, annual certifications, and five-year retention metadata.

steps.step_description: Collect the access-person roster, broker-feed coverage, report package, and acknowledgment log into one normalized packet before exception scoring begins.

automation_fit.rationale: Packet assembly is recurring, checklist-driven work that can normalize the same record types each cycle.

human_signoff_need.rationale: A reviewer should inspect gaps, but this intake step does not decide whether conduct violated the code.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: An incomplete packet creates rework and downstream review risk, but it is still an early-stage control rather than the final disciplinary call.

evidence_strength.rationale: The input set is directly anchored to rule-level reporting, acknowledgment, and recordkeeping obligations.

evidence_notes.claim: Rule 204A-1 and the recordkeeping amendments require advisers to collect and review access-person reports and acknowledgments, which supports a standardized intake packet.
SEC staff observed stronger programs where duplicate confirms, brokerage statements, and centralized monitoring were already pulled into one compliance process.
Exams cited missing review evidence and missing acknowledgments, so packet completeness reduces the risk of missing required follow-up later in the cycle.
Current examinations still test whether compliance programs are implemented and enforced, which favors a documented intake control even though the packet owner is not the final decision-maker.

steps.build_implication: Build a repeatable intake rail that assembles one review packet and exposes missing inputs before any human exception analysis starts.

steps.step_name: Confirm access-person scope and reporting obligations
steps.step_description: Review the role matrix, related entities, and account coverage to confirm who is an access person, which accounts are reportable, and which cases need explicit reviewer judgment.

automation_fit.rationale: Core definitions are rule-based, but edge cases depend on firm structure, role design, and access to recommendations or nonpublic information.

human_signoff_need.rationale: Someone accountable for the code should confirm edge-role classification because misclassifying access persons creates surveillance blind spots.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: If the wrong people are scoped out, required reports and preclearance obligations can be missed for an extended period.

evidence_strength.rationale: The rule text and exam findings provide clear anchors for who should be supervised and why this control matters.

evidence_notes.claim: EXAMS observed advisers that failed to identify and supervise certain employees as access persons, making classification accuracy a real compliance risk.
The adopting release ties access-person status to access to client recommendations, holdings, and other nonpublic information, which makes named reviewer judgment important for borderline roles.
The SEC highlighted failures to identify all access persons as a frequent code-of-ethics exam issue, reinforcing the downside of over-automating edge classifications.

Current exam priorities still focus on whether policies are actually tailored to the adviser’s operations, which means a tool can structure the decision but should not silently decide every borderline case.

steps.build_implication: Build a reviewer-visible access-person classifier that flags edge cases and records the basis for inclusion or exclusion instead of making hidden scope decisions.

steps.step_name: Reconcile missing, late, and incomplete reports
steps.step_description: Compare required holdings reports, quarterly trade reports, and acknowledgments against what was actually received and time-stamp each resulting exception.

automation_fit.rationale: This is rule-driven exception detection against known due dates, required report types, and acknowledgment requirements.

human_signoff_need.rationale: A human should review escalated exceptions, but the matching of expected versus received reports is mostly mechanical.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: Missing a report exception matters, but the resulting issue can still be checked and corrected before discipline or disclosure decisions are made.

evidence_strength.rationale: The timing, content, and review obligations are explicitly described in rule and exam materials.

evidence_notes.claim: Rule 204A-1 requires periodic holdings and transaction reporting plus adviser review, which creates a clean expected-versus-received exception check.

SEC staff observed effective programs using duplicate confirms, electronic records, and reviewer controls such as not permitting the reviewer to self-review, which supports automated exception detection with human escalation.
Untimely, missing, or unreviewed access-person reports were highlighted as common exam deficiencies, so catching them early has clear supervisory value.
EXAMS cited late submissions and lack of evidence of supervisory review, which means an automated exception queue directly addresses a recurring deficiency pattern.

steps.build_implication: Build a rules-driven exception engine for late reports, missing acknowledgments, and incomplete content before spending effort on a heavier reviewer experience.

steps.step_name: Review trades, preclearance, and restricted-list exceptions
steps.step_description: Compare flagged employee trades and preclearance requests against restricted lists, blackout controls, and client trading context to decide whether the exception is real and material.

automation_fit.rationale: The system can line up dates, lists, and reported trades, but the significance of a potential conflict still depends on context and supervisory interpretation.

human_signoff_need.rationale: A named reviewer should determine whether a flagged trade reflects a true code issue, an approved exception, or a harmless mismatch.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: Misreading a restricted-list or client-conflict exception can lead to overlooked fiduciary issues or unnecessary disciplinary action.

evidence_strength.rationale: The SEC sources are explicit about preclearance, restricted-list controls, and personal-trading conflict disclosure.

evidence_notes.claim: The adopting release requires pre-approval for IPOs and limited offerings and links personal-trading controls to fiduciary obligations, which makes this review step high consequence.

SEC staff observed effective programs maintaining current restricted and watch lists, enforcing blackout periods, and comparing preclearance forms to actual trading, which makes the comparison work highly structureable.
EXAMS observed employees trading restricted securities and missing pre-approval controls, so flagged trades should stay reviewer-visible rather than being auto-cleared.
Form ADV Part 2 requires firms to describe how they address conflicts from adviser and related-person personal trading, which raises the cost of an incorrect reviewer conclusion.

steps.build_implication: Build a reviewer workspace that assembles restricted-list, preclearance, and client-trade context for each flag instead of auto-concluding that every hit is a violation.

steps.step_name: Draft the exception memo and remediation package
steps.step_description: Turn the reviewed exceptions into a reviewer-editable memo covering facts, likely policy issue, proposed remediation, training needs, and any disclosure follow-up.

automation_fit.rationale: A tool can draft the packet structure and suggested follow-ups, but the narrative still depends on how the reviewer frames the issue and remediation.

human_signoff_need.rationale: The memo should remain reviewer-owned because it becomes the bridge between monitoring output and formal supervisory action.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: A bad memo can understate a real problem, overstate a minor one, or miss a needed disclosure or training response.

evidence_strength.rationale: The content can be anchored in rule obligations, exam findings, and brochure disclosure requirements.

evidence_notes.claim: The SEC requires records of code violations and actions taken, which makes the memo and remediation packet a structured supervisory output rather than optional narrative.
Observed effective practices included documented exceptions, sanctions, annual certifications, and training, which gives the memo a consistent structure even when the facts vary.
The 2022 alert notes that firms modified their codes and procedures after deficiencies were identified, so the remediation package should prepare judgment rather than silently resolve the issue.

Because the brochure must accurately describe code-of-ethics and personal-trading conflict controls, the memo should explicitly flag when an exception may require disclosure cleanup or consistency checks.

steps.build_implication: Build reviewer-editable memo drafts and remediation suggestions, but keep conclusions, tone, and any disclosure call fully in human hands.

steps.step_description: The chief compliance officer decides whether the exception is a violation, a documented waiver, or a no-issue event, and assigns any sanction, disclosure, or escalation.

automation_fit.rationale: The system can organize the evidence, but the supervisory judgment about sanctions, waivers, and escalation should not be automated.

human_signoff_need.rationale: This is the named supervisory decision point for whether the code was violated and what the consequence should be.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: A wrong call here can create disciplinary, disclosure, and books-and-records harm in both directions.

evidence_strength.rationale: The SEC materials clearly anchor the need for review, enforcement, and retention even though they do not mechanize the disciplinary judgment itself.

evidence_notes.claim: The adopting release expects the CCO or persons under the CCO’s authority to enforce the code and review access-person reports, which makes final adjudication an accountable human responsibility.
SEC staff observed that reviewers should not self-review and that exceptions and reprimands should be documented, so tooling should package evidence but not replace the decision-maker.
The 2022 alert cites weak supervisory review and self-review failures, which is a direct reason to keep the final violation call with a named reviewer.
Current examinations still test whether compliance policies are implemented and enforced, so the final sanctions or waiver decision should remain visibly owned by the accountable human reviewer.

steps.build_implication: Keep any product surface here limited to evidence packaging, prior-decision context, and audit traceability; do not automate the actual violation or sanctions decision.

steps.step_description: Store the final decision, update the violation log, queue training or disclosure follow-up, and preserve the records needed for future exams and annual compliance review.

automation_fit.rationale: Once a human decision exists, retention, certification reminders, and follow-up task creation are highly structured and repeatable.

human_signoff_need.rationale: The judgment has already happened; this step is mainly about complete retention and operational follow-through.

liability_asymmetry.rationale: Bad recordkeeping still matters, but the main disciplinary judgment should already be fixed before archival automation runs.

evidence_strength.rationale: The SEC sources are clear about keeping records of code violations, acknowledgments, reports, and decisions for later review.

evidence_notes.claim: The amended recordkeeping rule requires advisers to retain the code, violations and actions taken, written acknowledgments, access-person reports, and waiver decisions, making archival mechanics highly structureable.
SEC staff observed stronger programs where records, annual certifications, documented exceptions, and electronic analysis history were preserved for later review.
The 2022 alert cites firms that could not produce evidence of review or acknowledgments, which makes good retention an important control even after the substantive decision is over.
Because examinations continue to assess whether compliance programs are implemented and whether annual reviews are effective, post-decision records should be easy to retrieve and trace.

steps.build_implication: Build retention, certification, and follow-up automation after the human decision is logged so the supervisory record is exam-ready without adding new judgment risk.
